planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cotton lavender is a classic herb garden and knot garden plant excellent for low hedging and edging.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cotton lavender requires superb drainage — it will rot in wet or waterlogged soil and performs poorly in humid climates without excellent drainage. Santolina chamaecyparissus is a low, aromatic, silvery-gray, evergreen Mediterranean subshrub with dense, coral-like, deeply cut foliage that emits a strong, somewhat pungent, herbal scent when brushed; it produces small, button-like, bright-yellow flower heads on long, slender stems in mid-summer. In the traditional European herb garden, it was used as a low hedging plant in knot gardens and formal herb borders (the silver foliage contrasting beautifully with the green of boxwood and rosemary), and the dried leaves were used as a moth repellent for linen closets. Shear after flowering to maintain a neat, compact shape and prevent woodiness; takes well to clipping.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; green santolina has bright-green, more feathery foliage than gray santolina and is slightly more tolerant of moisture and humidity.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; green santolina requires the same excellent drainage and sunny, dry conditions as gray santolina but has slightly more tolerance of humidity and moisture. Santolina rosmarinifolia (syn. S. viridis) is a green-leaved counterpart to the familiar gray cotton lavender; it produces feathery, bright-green, rosemary-like foliage with a similar strong, herbal, camphor-like scent and equally bright-yellow button flowers in summer. The contrasting use of gray (S. chamaecyparissus) and green (S. rosmarinifolia) santolinas is a classic technique in traditional knot garden design; the green species' brighter, more vivid foliage makes it excellent as a companion to silver or purple-leaved plants in formal herb plantings. Shear after flowering to maintain shape.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Persian catmint is a low, mounding, sterile hybrid-like species excellent for border edging and as a groundcover in dry, sunny situations.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Persian catmint blooms most freely with good drainage and lean, somewhat dry soil — avoid rich, moist soil which produces lush but less floriferous plants. Nepeta mussinii is a low, aromatic, mounding European catmint with soft, gray-green, scalloped, strongly fragrant leaves and prolific spikes of small, lavender-blue flowers from late spring through early summer; shear plants by half after the first flush of bloom to stimulate a second flowering period in mid- to late summer. Unlike the commonly grown N. × faassenii (a sterile hybrid), N. mussinii is the true species from the Caucasus; it is slightly smaller than faassenii and sets viable seed. Attractive to bees, butterflies, and cats; resistant to deer and rabbits. Use as a low, billowing border edger or path-side planting.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; scarlet passionflower is frost-tender and must be grown in containers in zones 9 and below, or in frost-free zones 10–12.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in a frost-free location; in zone 9 and cooler, grow in large containers that can be moved indoors or to a greenhouse in winter. Passiflora vitifolia is a spectacular tropical passionflower vine native to Central and South America producing three-lobed, grape-like leaves (giving the species its name) and the showiest flowers in the genus — brilliant scarlet-red, 4-inch flowers with a striking corona of white-tipped, red filaments in the center; it blooms nearly continuously in tropical climates. The edible fruits are small, oblong, and yellow-green when ripe with a sweet-tart, grape-like flavor. It is one of the finest tropical passionflowers for greenhouse culture and large patio containers, with some of the most intensely colored and beautiful flowers in the genus; it requires warm temperatures year-round.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in frost-free conditions; red passionflower is an outstanding hummingbird plant in tropical and subtropical gardens.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in frost-free zones (10–12) or grow in containers that can be protected from frost in zones 9 and below. Passiflora coccinea is a Brazilian tropical passionflower vine with large, heart-shaped to deeply lobed, oblong leaves and brilliant scarlet, orange-red, or vermilion flowers with a prominent corona of white-banded filaments from late spring through fall in tropical climates; it is one of the finest plants for attracting hummingbirds in tropical gardens. The small, edible fruits are yellow-green when ripe with soft, sweet, aromatic pulp. Related to P. vitifolia but with somewhat different leaf shape and flower proportions; both are outstanding for tropical garden collections and large container culture. Excellent on arbors, fences, and trellises in frost-free climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; banana passionflower is more cold-hardy than most passionflowers and can survive light frost once established — it is considered invasive in Hawaii and some Pacific Islands, check local regulations.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; banana passionflower is more cold-tolerant than most passionflowers, surviving brief frosts to about 27°F when established, and has been successfully grown in sheltered spots in zone 8b and even zone 8a. Passiflora mollissima is an Andean native cultivated throughout the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia for its edible, banana-shaped, yellow, oblong fruits (curuba) 3–5 inches long with juicy, bright-orange, passion-fruit-flavored pulp used in juices, ice cream, and desserts; it is one of the most commercially important passionfruit species in South America. The flowers are beautiful — long-tubed, rose-pink to coral-pink, with a reduced corona — and are pollinated by hummingbirds. It grows vigorously and is considered invasive in warm, humid climates outside its native range (especially Hawaii, New Zealand, and South Africa); confirm it is legal to grow in your area.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops
University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
